coredata.dc:title: A magnetic imprinted polymer nano-adsorbent with embedded quantum dots and mesoporous carbon for the microextraction of triazine herbicides
coredata.dc:creator: Phirisi, Nurhasima
Płotka-Wasylka, Justyna
Bunkoed, Opas
coredata.dc:description: A magnetic molecularly imprinted polymer (MMIP) adsorbent incorporating amino-functionalized magnetite nanoparticles, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and mesoporous carbon (MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2) was fabricated to extract triazine herbicides from fruit juice. The embedded magnetite nanoparticles simplified the isolation of the adsorbent from the sample solution. The N-GQDs and MPC enhanced adsorption by affinity binding with triazines. The MIP layer provided highly specific recognition sites for the selective adsorption of three target triazines. The extracted triazines were determined by high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) coupled with diode-array detection (DAD). The developed method exhibited linearity from 1.5 to 100.0 µg L−1 with a detection limit of 0.5 µg L−1. Recoveries from spiked fruit juice samples were in the range of 80.1– 108.4 %, with a relative standard deviation of less than 6.0 %. The developed MMIP adsorbent demonstrated good selectivity, high extraction efficiency, ease of fabrication and use, and good stability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Triazine herbicides
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
Mesoporous carbons
Composite adsorbent
MIP

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The evaluation of the applicability of the procedure
Characterization of the nano-adsorbent and related materials
Optimization of the adsorbent fabrication and extraction condition
Type of nanocomposite adsorbent for the extraction of THs
Analysis of triazines in fruit juice
Evaluation of the applicability of the method
Comparison with a commercial adsorbent and other works

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the National Science, Research and Innovation Fund (NSRF) and Prince of Songkla University (Grant No. SCI6701211S ). Nurhasima Phirisi was supported by a Graduate Fellowship (Research Assistant) from the Faculty of Science, Prince of Songkla University , Contract no. 1-2565-02-029 . The authors thank Mr. Thomas Duncan Coyne for English proofreading.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The fabrication procedure of the MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2 adsorbent (A) and the procedure of d-MSPE with the fabricated nanocomposite adsorbent (B).

(A) Vibrating sample magnetometer curves are of (a) Fe3O4 NH2 and (b) MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2. The inset photograph shows the separation of the adsorbent from the sample solution with a magnet. (B) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2. (C) The XRD patterns are of (a) Fe3O4, (b) Fe3O4 NH2 and (c) MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2. (D) The weight loss of MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2 during thermal degradation was determined thermogravimetric analysis.
TEM (A) and SEM (B) images of MPC, TEM image of N-GQDs (C), SEM image of Fe3O4 (D), TEM (E) and SEM (F) images of MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2.
The recovery of different composite adsorbents toward three triazine herbicides.
The HPLC chromatograms of a juice sample (a), a spiked sample (b) and the standard three THs at 50.0 µg L−1(c).
The evaluation of the applicability of proposed methodology by the meaning of BAGI.
The analytical performance of the nanocomposite MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2 adsorbent for the extraction of THs in fruit juices.

The determination of THs in fruit juices using the MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4
NH2 adsorbent coupled with HPLC (mean ± RSD, n = 3).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: N.D.: not detected, <LOQ: lower limit of quantification.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A comparison of performances of analytical methods for the extraction and determination of triazines.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: ACOFX = Non-ionic silicone surfactant, AF-MCF = Amino-functionalized mesostructured cellular foam, M-DDM-CD-POF = Magnetic 4′4-diaminodiphenylmethane-cyclodextrin-porous organic framework, Fe-GIOMC = Magnetic N,S-codoped ordered mesoporous carbon, Fe3O4@SiO2GO/MIL101(Cr) = Magnetic grapheme oxide mesoporous silica composites, SUPRAS = Supramolecular solvent based on the surface-active ionic liquid, DMIP = Dual-template molecularly imprinted polymer, TAPA-Tp@MGO = Tris(4−aminophenyl) amine-2,4,6−triformylphloroglucinol magnetic graphene oxide, PDA= Photodiode-array detector.

head.ce:title: A magnetic imprinted polymer nano-adsorbent with embedded quantum dots and mesoporous carbon for the microextraction of triazine herbicides

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, Gdańsk University of Technology, 11/12G. Narutowicza Street, 80-233 Gdańsk, Poland

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: BioTechMed Center, Gdańsk University of Technology, 11/12G. Narutowicza Street, 80-233 Gdańsk, Poland
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: BioTechMed Center, Gdańsk University of Technology, 11/12G. Narutowicza Street, 80-233 Gdańsk, Poland

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A magnetic molecularly imprinted polymer (MMIP) adsorbent incorporating amino-functionalized magnetite nanoparticles, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and mesoporous carbon (MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2) was fabricated to extract triazine herbicides from fruit juice. The embedded magnetite nanoparticles simplified the isolation of the adsorbent from the sample solution. The N-GQDs and MPC enhanced adsorption by affinity binding with triazines. The MIP layer provided highly specific recognition sites for the selective adsorption of three target triazines. The extracted triazines were determined by high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) coupled with diode-array detection (DAD). The developed method exhibited linearity from 1.5 to 100.0 µg L−1 with a detection limit of 0.5 µg L−1. Recoveries from spiked fruit juice samples were in the range of 80.1– 108.4 %, with a relative standard deviation of less than 6.0 %. The developed MMIP adsorbent demonstrated good selectivity, high extraction efficiency, ease of fabrication and use, and good stability.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Triazine herbicides (THs) are utilized in agriculture to prevent the growth of grasses by inhibiting photosynthesis [1]. When applied excessively, THs leave residual traces in agricultural products that pose human health hazards such as skin problems, eye irritation, cancer and disruption of the endocrine system [2]. To protect consumer health, the European Union (EU) has stipulated a maximum residue limit (MRL) of THs in food products of 0.05 mg kg−1 [3]. Thus, the monitoring of THs in agricultural products is of some importance.

The determination of THs is mostly performed with HPLC technique, which is a highly sensitive and precise technique [4]. However, residual THs are normally found at trace levels in the presence of various interferences. Consequently, effective sample preparation techniques are required to enrich target analytes and reduce interferences prior to analysis. Sample preparation involves the extraction of target analytes, and several techniques are employed. Solid phase extraction (SPE) [5], solid phase microextraction (SPME) [6], fabric phase sorptive extraction (FPSE) [7], dispersive solid-phase extraction (dSPE) [8,9] and dispersive magnetic solid phase extraction (d-MSPE) [10,11] have all been successfully used, but the most conventional technique, SPE, is time consuming, complicated and not highly selective. For the determination of trace residues in aqueous samples, d-MSPE is an interesting alternative method of extracting and enriching THs. The procedure maximizes contact between the adsorbent and target analyte, and the loaded adsorbent can be simply isolated from sample solution with a magnet. In addition, d-MSPE requires less organic solvent than SPE and reduces extraction time [5]. Adsorbents used in d-MSPE processes generally utilize magnetite nanoparticles (Fe3O4) as a ferromagnetic solid support [12] but bare Fe3O4 nanoparticles easily aggregate and oxidize [13]. Therefore, Fe3O4 is usually modified to overcome these limitations. Amino-functionalized magnetite nanoparticles (Fe3O4 NH2) have been proposed to also facilitate dispersion and easy modification with other adsorption materials [14].

The extraction efficiency of d-MSPE is crucial for the determination of analytes from complex matrix compositions and normally depends on the effectiveness of the adsorbent. THs have been adsorbed on a metal organic framework [15], a porous organic polymer [16], graphene [17], a covalent organic framework [18] and a molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) [19]. MIP is an interesting material that features specific binding sites that retain the memory of the size, shape and functional groups of the template (target analytes). MIPs are generally prepared via the sol-gel polymerization of a monomer in the presence of a cross-linker and a template molecule, or sometimes more than one. The strategy has been widely applied and has achieved progress in many fields owing to the tunable selectivity, ease of preparation and physical robustness of MIP [20]. In addition, MIP can be incorporated with other adsorption materials to improve adsorption efficiency. Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and mesoporous carbon (MPC) have been used for this purpose. N-GQDs are structurally composed of single or multiple layers of graphene (G) with many functional hydroxyl and amine groups, which can enhance the dispersibility of the QDs in water. Their advantages also include low toxicity and biocompatibility [21,22]. MPC has pores that can range from 2 to 50 nm in diameter, a large surface area and a uniform pore size. All these properties could increase adsorption between the MIP adsorbent and THs [23]. MPC is typically highly hydrophobic due to the presence of nonpolar carbon atoms in its structure [24] and can therefore adsorb THs via hydrophobic interaction.

In this work, a novel composite adsorbent was fabricated by embedding Fe3O4, N-GQDs and MPC in MIP (MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2). The adsorbent was applied in the d-MSPE procedure to extract simazine, atrazine and propazine. The extracted THs were determined by HPLC-DAD. The nanocomposite MMIP could adsorb THs via hydrogen bonding, hydrophobic interaction and specific binding with recognition cavities of the MIP. The applicability of the developed method was evaluated by the application of the Blue Applicability Grade Index (BAGI). The novelty of the proposed method is the incorporation of N-GQDs and MPC into an MMIP adsorbent, which represents the first instance of this integration to display significant potential for the extraction of THs in food samples.

Simazine (SMZ) and propazine (PPZ) were purchased from LGC Standard (Augsburg, Germany). Atrazine (ATZ) was obtained from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co. Ltd. (Japan). 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) and tris-hydroxymethyl aminomethane (Tris-HMA) were obtained from Sigma Aldrich (Steinheim, Germany). MPC was from XFNANO (Jiangsu, China). NaOH was from Loba Chemie Pvt. Ltd. (Mumbai, India). Methanol, acetonitrile and ammonia solutions were from RCI Labscan (Bangkok, Thailand). FeCl2⋅4H2O and FeCl3⋅6H2O were from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Citric acid was from KemAus (Western, Australia).

The HPLC 1200 (Agilent Technologies, Germany) equipped with a Fortis C18 (150 mm × 4.6 mm I.D., 5.0 µm) column was used to separate THs. The isocratic elution was performed using water and acetonitrile as the mobile phase (50: 50 % v/v) at a flow rate of 0.90 mL min−1. THs were detected by the diode array at 220 nm. Infrared spectra were recorded by FTIR spectroscopy (PerkinElmer, USA). The morphologies of the adsorbent were examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Apreo, FEI, Netherlands) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JOEL, JEM 2010, Japan). The surface area of the adsorbent was investigated by the BET method using a surface area analyzer (Model ASAP 2060 Micromeritics, USA). The thermal stability was investigated by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA8000, PerkinElmer, USA). The magnetic properties were determined with a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) (Lakerhore 730908, USA). The crystalline phases of the adsorbent were investigated by X-ray diffractometry (XRD) (Empreyan, PAN Analytical, Netherlands). Zeta potentials were achieved by zeta potential analyzer (Zeta PALS, Brookhaven, USA)

Magnetite nanoparticles were synthesized by first combining 9.40 g of FeCl3⋅6H2O and 3.40 g of FeCl2⋅4H2O. The combined powders were dissolved with stirring under nitrogen gas in 160 mL of DI water at 80 °C. Then, 20 mL of NH4OH were added dropwise under continuous stirring. After 1 h, the solution changed color from orange to brown, indicating the formation of Fe3O4 nanoparticles (NPs). The synthesized Fe3O4 NPs were separated with a magnet, washed with deionized water and dried at 80 °C for 6 h. A dispersion of 0.50 g of the synthesized Fe3O4 NPs in 90 mL of a mixture solution of ethanol and DI water (50:50 % v/v) was sonicated for 30 min. Then, 0.70 mL of APTES was added, and the pH of the solution was adjusted to 4.0 with acetic acid. The mixture was heated at 60 °C for 3 h under stirring. The synthesized Fe3O4 NH2 NPs were washed with deionized water and dried at 70 °C for 8 h.

N-GQDs were synthesized by the pyrolysis of citric acid and Tris-HMA. 2.0 g of citric acid and 0.50 g of tris-HMA were mixed and melted at 225 °C for 15 min. A dark, solid product was obtained, which was adjusted to pH 7.0 with 1.0 M NaOH to obtain a N-GQDs solution [25].

The nanocomposite MMIP adsorbent was prepared via a sol–gel polymerization process. Briefly, 100 mg L−1 of simazine, atrazine and propazine were dissolved in 30 mL of methanol and 0.30 mL of APTES was added. The mixture solution was stirred at 400 rpm for 1 h. Then, 30 mg of MPC, 40 mg of as -synthesized Fe3O4 NH2 and 30 mL of as-synthesized N-GQDs were added, and the mixture was continuously stirred for 1 h. Next, 2.0 mL of TEOS and 0.90 mL of 25 % v/v ammonia solution were added and the mixture solution was continuously stirred for 24 h to complete polymerization. The obtained nanocomposite MMIP adsorbent was washed with 40 mL of methanol to remove the simazine, atrazine and propazine template molecules. A magnetic non-imprinted polymer (MNIP) adsorbent was also prepared using the same procedure but without the template. The fabrication of the MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2 is shown in Fig. 1 A.

First, 0.20 g of the MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2 adsorbent was placed in the sample solution and stirred for 30 min to adsorb THs. The external magnet was then applied to separate the adsorbent from the solution. The THs were desorbed from the adsorbent by sonication using methanol as the solvent. Finally, the desorption solvent (methanol) was evaporated. Before HPLC analysis, the residue was dissolved in 1.0 mL of the mobile phase (water and acetonitrile at 50: 50 % v/v) and passed through a 0.20 μm PTFE filter. The d-MSPE procedure is illustrated in Fig. 1B.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The evaluation of the applicability of the procedure
ce:section.ce:para: A blue applicability grade index (BAGI) was used to evaluate the practicality of the proposed analytical method. A desktop application was used to create the BAGI pictogram [26].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the nano-adsorbent and related materials
ce:section.ce:para: Functional groups of the MMIP adsorbent were identified by FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. S1). The FTIR spectrum of Fe3O4 NH2 showed a characteristic peak at 581 cm−1 due to the stretching vibration of FeO. The sharp peak at 1086 cm−1 was derived from the stretching of SiOSi and the peak at 3422 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of OH. The FTIR spectrum of N-GQDs presented a broad adsorption peak at 3422 cm−1 that corresponded to OH/NH and a peak at 2923 cm−1 that indicated CH stretching. The peak at 1578 cm−1 was due to the CC stretching vibration of the aromatic ring and the adsorption peaks at 1405 and 1272 cm−1 were derived from the stretching vibrations of CNHC and CO, respectively. The broad adsorption peak at 3422 cm−1 in the spectrum of MPC was due to OH/NH and the peak at 2918 cm−1 was due to CH stretching. The peak at 1587 cm−1 was due to the stretching of CC and the adsorption peaks at 1400 and 1271 cm−1 corresponded to stretching vibrations of CNHC and CO, respectively. The adsorption peaks of the spectrum of MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2 demonstrated that the nanocomposite MMIP adsorbent was successfully fabricated.

The magnetic properties of Fe3O4 NH2 and MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2 were measured with a vibrating sample magnetometer. The VSM curve of Fe3O4 NH2 indicated a maximum saturation of 63.51 emu g−1 and the VSM curve of MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2 indicated a maximum saturation of 14.83 emu g−1 (Fig. 2 A). The lower maximum saturation of MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2 was caused by the non-ferromagnetic polymer layer. However, the magnetization value of MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2 still represented a satisfactory magnetic response. The nanocomposite MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2 adsorbent could therefore be separated from the sample within 10 s.

The surface area and pore structure of MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2 were investigated by plotting nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms (Fig. 2B). MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2 exhibited type IV isotherm characteristics. The BET surface area of MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2 was 111 m2 g−1, which could provide a significant number of adsorption sites for the extraction of THs. The average pore diameter of MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2 was 15.52 nm, indicating a mesoporous structure.

XRD analysis identified the crystal structure of Fe3O4, Fe3O4 NH2 and MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2. The XRD patterns of Fe3O4 NH2 and MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2 materials presented diffraction peaks at 30°, 35°, 43°, 54°, 57° and 63° 2θ (Fig. 2C), which corresponded to the characteristic peaks of Fe3O4 [27]. The results indicated that Fe3O4 NH2 nanoparticles were successfully embedded in the nanocomposite MMIP adsorbent.

The thermal stability of MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2 was investigated using TGA. The TGA curve above 100 °C showed the loss of residual moisture in the adsorbent (Fig. 2D). Above 400 °C, the weight of the nanocomposite decreased due to the loss of the polymer. The analysis indicated that the thermal stability of MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2 was suitable for the d-MSPE procedure at room temperature (∼25–30 °C).

The surface morphology and microstructure of the composite materials were examined using FESEM and TEM. The TEM and SEM images of MPC showed a uniformly worm-like (Fig. 3 A) and layered morphology (Fig. 3B). TEM image of N-GQDs (Fig. 3C) and SEM image of Fe3O4 (Fig.3D) showed exhibited monodisperse nanoparticles with spherical shape. The TEM (Fig. 3E) and SEM (Fig. 3F) images of MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2 revealed the rough surfaces and uniform arrangement of adsorption materials in the composite adsorbent.

The chemical composition of MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2 was analyzed using EDS. The results revealed the presence of C, N, O, Si and Fe elements in the adsorbent (Fig. S2). The presence of Si was attributed to the polymer layer of MIP. The N, Fe and O elements were attributed to the amino-functionalized magnetite nanoparticles, and C and N were attributed to MPC and N-GQDs, respectively. The EDS analysis confirmed the successful synthesis of MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the adsorbent fabrication and extraction condition
ce:section.ce:para: The parameters for the fabrication of the MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2 adsorbent and extraction of THs were optimized to achieve the best extraction efficiency in the shortest analysis time with low reagent consumption. The results are summarized in Table S1. The optimization was performed by extracting THs spiked in DI water to give a concentration of 50 µg L−1.

The adsorbent dosage influences the availability of adsorption sites. The dosage of the MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2 adsorbent was studied at 75.0, 100.0, 200.0 and 300.0 mg. The extraction of THs increased with increments of adsorbent from 75.0 mg to 200.0 mg (Fig. S3A). Therefore, a dosage of 200.0 mg of the adsorbent was sufficient to adsorb the target THs, providing the most efficient extraction.

The amount of N-GQDs embedded in a composite MIP adsorbent plays a crucial role in the synthesis of the material. N-GQDs can interact with template, contributing to the formation of the imprinted polymer layer and enhancing the mass yield of the MIP layer. MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2 was synthesized using 20.0, 25.0, 30.0, 35.0 and 40.0 mL of N-GQDs solution. The extraction efficiency of the nanocomposite MMIP adsorbent was highest when 25.0 mL was used (Fig. S3B). Therefore, 25.0 mL of N-GQDs solution was chosen for the synthesis of the MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2 adsorbent.

The appropriate extraction time was necessary to allow the complete adsorption of THs for enhanced extraction efficiency. The extraction time was evaluated from 10 to 40 min. Extraction efficiency exhibited a gradual improvement from 20 to 30 min and then remained stable, indicating that THs were completely adsorbed within 30 min. Consequently, 30 min was selected as the optimal extraction time.

The stirring rate of the sample solution in the d-MSPE procedure influences mass transfer and contact time between the target THs and adsorbent. In this work, the stirring rate of was investigated from 500 to 1500 rpm. Extraction efficiency was highest when the stirring rate was 800 rpm and decreased at faster stirring rates (Fig. S3D). Low stirring rates and an excessively high stirring rate could reduce the contact between the THs and adsorbent, reducing extraction efficiency [28]. Thus, a stirring rate of 800 rpm was chosen.

The pH of the sample affects extraction efficiency by influencing the ionization and solubility of analytes in the sample. Sample solution pH in this study was investigated in the range of 3.0–11.0 by adjusting with HCl or NaOH solutions. Sample solutions at pH 5.0–7.0 produced high extraction efficiencies (Fig. S3E). Under strong acidic conditions, THs were present in the cationic state and the adsorbent surface became positively charged, resulting in electrostatic repulsion between the analytes and the adsorbent. In strong alkaline conditions, both the adsorbent surface and the structure of THs were deprotonated and shifted to anionic states. The zeta potentials of the MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2 adsorbent are given in Fig. S3F. The isoelectric point (IEP) of MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2 adsorbent was measured to be ∼ 7.6. The developed adsorbent is positively charged at pH values below the IEP and negatively charged above the IEP. Also, the polymer layer became unstable in the alkaline condition, reducing the binding between THs and the adsorbent [29]. Since the pH of the tested fruit juice samples was between 5.0 and 7.0, the sample pH did not need to be adjusted.

The ionic strength is a crucial factor that can impact extraction efficiency by influencing the solubility of THs in an aqueous solution and mass transfer to the adsorbent. The results indicated that extraction efficiency was unaffected by the addition of NaCl (Fig. S4). However, the increased viscosity of the solution caused by the addition of NaCl reduced the mass transfer rate of THs to the adsorbent and extraction efficiency was low [30]. Therefore, the extraction of THs was performed without the addition of NaCl.

The THs adsorbed on the nanocomposite MMIP adsorbent were desorbed using methanol, acetonitrile, hexane, a mixture of methanol and acetonitrile and a mixture of methanol and acetic acid. Methanol provided the most effective desorption (Fig. S5A). Another essential parameter is the desorption solvent volume. The volume of methanol used to desorb the target analytes was varied at 1.0, 3.0, 5.0, 10.0, and 15.0 mL. Extraction efficiency slightly increased as the volume increased from 1.0 to 5.0 mL and remained stable at bigger solvent volumes (Fig. S5B). Therefore, 5.0 mL was chosen as the most suitable volume of methanol to desorb the analytes of interest. Desorption time is an important parameter that allows the most complete desorption of THs from the adsorbent. Desorption with ultrasonication was evaluated from 5 to 40 min. Extraction efficiency gradually increased from 5 to 20 min and then remained almost unchanged at 30 min (Fig. S5C). Extraction efficiency was low at 40 min due to the adsorption of desorbed THs back onto the adsorbent. Therefore, 20 min was chosen to desorb the three THs from the adsorbent.

To improve the extraction efficiency of the nanocomposite MMIP adsorbent, MPC was incorporated to enhance adsorption through hydrophobic interaction and hydrogen bonding. The amount of MPC incorporated was varied from 10.0 to 50.0 mg. Extraction efficiency increased with increments of MPC loading from 10.0 to 30.0 mg and remained constant at higher loadings (Fig. 5D). MPC typically has a large surface area that provides a large active surface for the adsorption of template molecules. After the removal of the template molecules, a large number of recognition site were formed to adsorb THs, which improved extraction efficiency.

The sample volume plays a significant role in the extraction and the enrichment of analytes. In this work, the sample volume was varied from 5.0 to 20.0 mL. Extraction efficiency slightly decreased when the sample volume was more than 10.0 mL (Fig. S6). The results suggested that larger sample volumes could exceed the adsorption capacity of the adsorbent or reduce the contact rate between analyte and adsorbent. The enrichment factor was higher for a 10.0 mL sample volume than a 5.0 mL volume. To ensure sufficient interaction between the THs and the nanocomposite MMIP adsorbent, a sample volume of 10.0 mL was considered the optimal condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Type of nanocomposite adsorbent for the extraction of THs
ce:section.ce:para: The three target THs were extracted with different adsorbents in the optimized extraction condition. The applied adsorbent composites included NIP@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2, MIP@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2, NIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2 and MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2 (Fig. 4 ). The NIP@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2and MIP@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2produced lower extraction recoveries than the MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2 because, without MPC, the adsorbents could not adsorb sufficient amounts of THs. The MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2 adsorbent recovered more than 80 % of the target anlytes. The adsorption of THs by the developed composite adsorbent was primarily dependent on the imprinted recognition sites, hydrophobic property of MPC, and hydrogen bonding.

The adsorption of THs on the MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2 adsorbent was evaluated using the pseudo-first order (PFO) and pseudo-second order (PSO) models. The results (Table S2) revealed that the R2 value of the PSO model was greater than that of the PFO model, with an R2 ≥ 0.99, indicating that the adsorption process on the MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2 adsorbent was primarily controlled by chemisorption. Both kinetic models were evaluated according to the following equations [31]:
(1)
PFO ;
log
(
q e
−
q t
)
= log
q e
−
K 1
2.303
t
(2)
PSO ;
t
q t
=
1
K
q e 2
+
1
q e
t
where, Qe (mg g−1) represents the adsorption capacity of THs on the adsorbent at equilibrium, while Qt (mg g−1) indicates the adsorption capacity at time t (min). K1 (min⁻¹) and K2 (g mg⁻¹ min⁻¹) are the rate constant of the PFO and PSO models, respectively.

The performances of the developed MMIP adsorbent were assessed in terms of linear range, coefficient of determination (R2), limits of detection (LODs), limits of quantification (LOQs) and precision. THs in fruit samples were extracted in the optimum condition and analyzed with HPLC-DAD. The linear range for simazine was from 1.5 to 100.0 μg L−1. The linear ranges for atrazine and propazine were both from 3.0 to 100.0 μg L−1 (Table 1 ). All three R2 were greater than 0.998. LODs and LOQs were determined based on a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 and 10, respectively. The developed method produced LODs ranging from 0.5 to 1.0 μg L−1 and LOQs ranging from 1.5 to 3.0 μg L−1. The enrichment factor (EF) was calculated using the equation EF = Cf/Ci, where Cf and Ci is the concentration of THs in the sample and reconstituted solvent, respectively. The EF was about 9.9. The RSDs were lower than 6 %, demonstrating good precision for the extraction and determination of THs in fruit juices.

Due to the complexity of fruit juice samples, interferences may affect the quantitative analysis of THs and the accuracy of the analytical results. Therefore, the matrix effect (ME) was evaluated by comparing the calibration curves of THs in spiked fruit juice samples and spiked methanol in different conditions. The ME was calculated following the equation [32]: ME = ((slope of calibration curve of spiked fruit juices/slope of calibration curve of spiked solvent) – 1) × 100. MEs were in the range of −1.5 % to −9.6 % (Table S3). These results indicated that the matrix effect was low after extraction with the developed method. However, to ensure the accuracy of analytical results, the matrix-matched calibration was employed for the determination of THs in fruit juices.

The selectivity of MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2 adsorbent was also investigated using terbumeton and terbuthylazine as non-target analytes which have similar structures to target analytes. As shown in Fig. S7, the extraction recoveries of target analytes were achieved between 83 % and 98 %, while the recoveries of terbumeton and terbuthylazine were achieved between 16 and 18 %. The results demonstrated that the developed MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2 adsorbent is highly selective to target analytes (template). The developed composite adsorbent can adsorb other compounds via only non-specific binding.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of triazines in fruit juice
ce:section.ce:para: The developed nanocomposite MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2 adsorbent coupled with HPLC-DAD was utilized to extract and determine THs in fruit juices. Fruit juices sample including orange, grape, guava, tomato and coconut were obtained from local supermarkets. 50 mL of the juice samples were centrifuged at 5000 rpm and the supernatant was separated, diluted to 250 mL with DI water. They were filtered through 0.20 μm PTFE filter before applied to d-MSPE extraction. Traces of THs were found at a lower quantification in real samples except tomato and coconut (Table 2 ). The accuracy was evaluated by spiking standard solution of THs at concentrations of 10.0, 20.0 and 50.0 µg L−1 in real sample. The relative recovery (RR) was employed for accuracy assessment, calculated using the following equation: RR (%) = (CT-CS)/CSpiked)) × 100. Where CT represents the total concentration of THs found in the real sample after spiking with the standard, CS is the initial concentration of THs in the real sample and CSpiked is the concentration of standard THs spiked in the sample. The relative recovery of the fruit juice samples ranged from 80.1 to 108.4 % with RSDs less than 6 %. The HPLC chromatogram of real samples, spiked samples and the standard THs are shown in Fig. 5 . The peaks of THs were clearly separated from interference peaks. The results demonstrated that the developed method based on d-MSPE using nanocomposite MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2 adsorbent had good applicability for the determination of THs in fruit juices.

The reusability of the fabricated MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2 adsorbent was evaluated through repeated adsorption-desorption experiments of THs using the same adsorbent. After each d-MSPE procedure, the used MMIP adsorbent was cleaned with methanol and DI water to eliminate the carry-over effect. As illustrated in Fig. S8A, the adsorbent can be reused for 6 extraction cycles, provided the recovery was greater than 80 %. The decreasing of extraction efficiency after 6 cycles may be due to the breaking of the polymer layer and adsorption material from the composite adsorbent during the adsorption-desorption experiments and washing steps. The SEM was further evaluated to confirm the structure of the composite adsorbent after reuse for 6 cycles. The result demonstrated that the morphology of the recycled adsorbent exhibited an irregular shape and a decreased rough surface compared to the initial adsorbent (Fig. S8B and S8C). The results indicated that the adsorbent could be reused for six extraction cycles with satisfactory recovery. The stability of the composite adsorbent in desorption solvent (methanol) and water at pH 6–7 were also evaluated for 2 weeks. The extraction efficiency of the target analytes is still greater than 80 % which can confirm the stability of the MIP@MPC@N-GQDs@Fe3O4 NH2 adsorbent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the applicability of the method
ce:section.ce:para: BAGI has been used to assess the applicability of the proposed procedure. This index revolves around the “blue” principles of white analytical chemistry [33], which are mainly related to practical aspects. BAGI pictogram represents ten criteria. In addition to created visual representation, it produces a score that depicts the applicability and functionality of evaluated analytical method. A sequential blue colour scale was applied to present the final score [34]. The obtained total score allows to if the analytical method can be considered “practical” and also, follow the parameters which should be improved. The BAGI pictogram generated for the procedure presented in this study is shown in Fig. 6 .

The information that can be found by application of the given method was both quantitative and confirmatory due to the employment of the DAD detector that was set at 220 nm. The determination enabled the quantification of three THs belonging to one class (herbicides). Since the adsorbent is not commercially available, it needs to be prepared in the lab in a relatively simple and straightforward way using simple equipment. Regarding the instrumentation, simple equipment available in most labs was used. The simultaneous sample preparation of many samples can be assumed. Following the simultaneous sample preparation of the 10 samples that requires a time span of around 30 min, the total analysis time by HPLC is 20 min, resulting in a sample throughput of 2.6 h−1. Semi-automated analysis was performed, however, the manual treatment of the sample was required for some parts, which can be considered a drawback of the method. This aspect of the method could be improved by automating some steps of the sample preparation. As for the prepared sample, miniaturized extraction was used and the sample volume for the bioanalytical matrix was 10 mL. In sum, a BAGI score of 67.5 was attained for the method and the whole procedure is potentially applicable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison with a commercial adsorbent and other works

ce:section.ce:para: A MMIP adsorbent was successfully developed by incorporating amino-functionalized magnetite nanoparticles, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and mesoporous carbon (MPC) into molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP). The fabricated nanocomposite MMIP adsorbent was employed to extract triazine herbicides from fruit juice samples through the interaction of hydrogen bonding, hydrophobic and specific recognition cavities of MIP. The developed nanocomposite adsorbent demonstrated a high adsorption capacity, significant selectivity and good stability. The developed nanocomposite adsorbent exhibited high extraction efficiency and ease of fabrication making it suitable for the determination of triazines in food samples. The proposed strategy can be applied t to extract other compounds in complex samples by changing the template molecules.

Nurhasima Phirisi: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Justyna Płotka-Wasylka: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Opas Bunkoed: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Science, Research and Innovation Fund (NSRF) and Prince of Songkla University (Grant No. SCI6701211S). Nurhasima Phirisi was supported by a Graduate Fellowship (Research Assistant) from the Faculty of Science, Prince of Songkla University, Contract no. 1-2565-02-029. The authors thank Mr. Thomas Duncan Coyne for English proofreading.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.chroma.2024.464977.
